is the southern [subregion](/wiki/Subregion#Asia "Subregion") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") that is defined in both geographical and [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity")-[cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture") terms. South Asia, with a population of 2.04 billion, contains a quarter (25%) of the world's population. As commonly conceptualised, the modern [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") of South Asia include [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") also often included, which may also be classified as part of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaez201258-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8) South Asia borders [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") to the northeast, Central Asia to the northwest, [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia") to the west and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") to the east. [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [Maritime South Asia](/wiki/Littoral_South_Asia "Littoral South Asia") are the only subregions of Asia that lie partly within the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"). The [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory") and two out of [26 atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_Maldives "Atolls of Maldives") of the Maldives in South Asia lie entirely within the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"). Topographically, it is dominated by the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and is bounded by the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") in the south, and the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"), [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram"), and [Pamir Mountains](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") in the north.[[8]](#cite_note-Chapman,_Graham_P._p._10-9)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Subregion of the Asian continent

This article is about the geographical subregion of Asia. For the physiographical region of Eurasia, see [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). For The Indian cultural sphere, see [Greater India](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India").

For other uses, see [Southern Asia](/wiki/Southern_Asia_(disambiguation) "Southern Asia (disambiguation)").

**South Asia** is the southern [subregion](/wiki/Subregion#Asia "Subregion") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") that is defined in both geographical and [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity")-[cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture") terms. South Asia, with a population of 2.04 billion, contains a quarter (25%) of the world's population. As commonly conceptualised, the modern [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") of South Asia include [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") also often included, which may also be classified as part of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaez201258-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8) South Asia borders [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") to the northeast, Central Asia to the northwest, [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia") to the west and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") to the east. [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [Maritime South Asia](/wiki/Littoral_South_Asia "Littoral South Asia") are the only subregions of Asia that lie partly within the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"). The [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory") and two out of [26 atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_Maldives "Atolls of Maldives") of the Maldives in South Asia lie entirely within the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"). Topographically, it is dominated by the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and is bounded by the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") in the south, and the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"), [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram"), and [Pamir Mountains](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") in the north.[[8]](#cite_note-Chapman,_Graham_P._p._10-9)

[Settled life](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") emerged on the Indian subcontinent in the western margins of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") [Basin](/wiki/Indus_Basin "Indus Basin") 9,000 years ago, evolving gradually into the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") of the third millennium BCE.[[9]](#cite_note-Combined-2-10) By 1200 BCE, an [archaic form](/wiki/Proto-language "Proto-language") of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), an [Indo-European language](/wiki/Indo-European_language "Indo-European language"), had [diffused](/wiki/Trans-cultural_diffusion "Trans-cultural diffusion") into India from the northwest,[[10]](#cite_note-Lowe2015-11)[[11]](#cite_note-Combined-4-Rigveda2-12) with the [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") being supplanted in the northern and western regions.[[12]](#cite_note-Combined-4-13) By 400 BCE, [stratification](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification") and [exclusion](/wiki/Social_exclusion "Social exclusion") by [caste](/wiki/Caste "Caste") had emerged within [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"),[[13]](#cite_note-Dyson2018-16a-14) and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") had arisen, proclaiming [social orders](/wiki/Social_order "Social order") unlinked to heredity.[[14]](#cite_note-Fisher2018-59-15)

In the early medieval era, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") became established on South Asia's southern and western coasts.[[15]](#cite_note-Combined-8-16) [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") armies from Central Asia intermittently overran the plains of northern India,[[16]](#cite_note-Combined-13-17) eventually founding the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") in the 13th century, and drawing the region into the cosmopolitan [networks of medieval Islam](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age").[[17]](#cite_note-Combined-10-18) The Islamic [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), in 1526, ushered in two centuries of relative peace,[[18]](#cite_note-AsherAsher2006-152-19) leaving a legacy of luminous architecture.[[a]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-Fisher2018-106-20) Gradually expanding [rule of the British East India Company](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") followed, turning most of South Asia into a colonial economy, but also consolidating its [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty").[[20]](#cite_note-Combined-11-22) [British Crown rule](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") began in 1858. The rights promised to Indians were granted slowly,[[21]](#cite_note-Combined-12-23)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeers2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksiddyQuAgAAQBAJpgPA76_76]-24) but [technological changes](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") were introduced, and modern ideas of education and the public life took root.[[23]](#cite_note-EmbreeHay1988-25) In 1947, the British Indian Empire was [partitioned](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") into two independent [dominions](/wiki/Dominion#India_and_Pakistan "Dominion"),[[24]](#cite_note-fisher-partition-26)[[25]](#cite_note-chatterji-partition-27)[[26]](#cite_note-talbot-sing-28)[[27]](#cite_note-khan-great-partition-29) a Hindu-majority [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and a Muslim-majority [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan"), amid large-scale loss of life and an unprecedented migration.[[28]](#cite_note-30) The 1971 [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War"), a [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War_in_Asia#South_Asia "Cold War in Asia") episode resulting in [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan")'s secession,[[29]](#cite_note-31) was the most recent instance of a new nation being formed in the region.

South Asia has a total area of 5.2 million km2 (2 million sq.mi), which is 10% of the Asian continent.[[30]](#cite_note-EoMA-32) The population of South Asia is estimated to be 2.04 billion[[8]](#cite_note-Chapman,_Graham_P._p._10-9) or about one-fourth of the world's population, making it both the [most populous](/wiki/List_of_continents_and_continental_subregions_by_population "List of continents and continental subregions by population") and the [most densely populated](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") geographical region in the world.[[31]](#cite_note-South_Asia_Regional_Overview-33)

In 2022, South Asia had the world's largest populations of [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus"), [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs"), Jains, and Zoroastrians.[[32]](#cite_note-dip8Jan2016-34) South Asia alone accounts for 90.47% of Hindus, 95.5% of Sikhs, and 31% of Muslims worldwide, as well as 35 million [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") and 25 million Buddhists.[[33]](#cite_note-Pew_Research_2012-35)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERuffle2013193-36)[[35]](#cite_note-pewforum.org-37)[[36]](#cite_note-pew2Apr2015-38)

The [South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation") (SAARC) is an economic cooperation organisation in the region which was established in 1985 and includes all of the South Asian nations.[[37]](#cite_note-SAARC_Summit-39)

## Definition

### Ambiguity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/South_Asia_%28ed%29update.PNG/500px-South_Asia_%28ed%29update.PNG)](/wiki/File:South_Asia_(ed)update.PNG)

Various definitions of South Asia, including the definition by the [United Nations geoscheme](/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme "United Nations geoscheme") which was created for "statistical convenience and does not imply any assumption regarding political or other affiliation of countries or territories."[[38]](#cite_note-Millenniumindicators.un.org-40)

The geographical extent is not clear cut as systemic and foreign policy orientations of its constituents are quite asymmetrical.[[39]](#cite_note-Ghosh1989-41) Beyond the core territories of the Indian Empire (territories of the British Empire which were under the system of British Raj), there is a high degree of variation as to which other countries are included in South Asia.[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-Razzaque3-43)[[42]](#cite_note-mmann-44)[[43]](#cite_note-anderson5-45) There is no clear boundary – geographical, geopolitical, socio-cultural, economical, or historical – between South Asia and other parts of Asia, especially Southeast Asia and West Asia.[[44]](#cite_note-46) Most of this region is peninsular, resting on the [Indian Plate](/wiki/Indian_Plate "Indian Plate") and being isolated from the rest of Asia by mountain barriers;[[45]](#cite_note-47)[[46]](#cite_note-48) it resembles a diamond which is delineated by the Himalayas on the north, the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") in the west, and the [Arakanese](/wiki/Chin_Hills "Chin Hills") in the east,[[47]](#cite_note-Baker2002-49) and which extends southward into the Indian Ocean with the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") to the southwest and the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") to the southeast.[[48]](#cite_note-Oxford-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/South_Asia_%28orthographic_projection%29_without_national_boundaries%2C_with_ambiguities_indicated.svg/120px-South_Asia_%28orthographic_projection%29_without_national_boundaries%2C_with_ambiguities_indicated.svg.png)](/wiki/File:South_Asia_(orthographic_projection)_without_national_boundaries,_with_ambiguities_indicated.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Map_of_Central_Asia.svg/120px-Map_of_Central_Asia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Central_Asia.svg)

The [northwestern boundary](/wiki/Northwestern_South_Asia "Northwestern South Asia") of South Asia is debated, with Afghanistan being variously described as South or Central Asian

The common definition of South Asia is largely inherited from the administrative boundaries of the Indian Empire,[[50]](#cite_note-52) with several exceptions. The current territories of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), and Pakistan which were the core territories of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") from 1857 to 1947 also form the core territories of South Asia.[[51]](#cite_note-The_World_Bank-53)[[52]](#cite_note-eldis.org-54) The mountain countries of [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), two independent countries that were not under the British Raj but were protectorates of the Empire,[[53]](#cite_note-55) and the [island countries](/wiki/Island_South_Asia "Island South Asia") of Sri Lanka and the Maldives are generally included.[[54]](#cite_note-McLeod2002-56) By various definitions based on substantially different reasons, the [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory") and the [Tibet Autonomous Region](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region") may be included as well.[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62) [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") (Burma), a former British colony and now largely considered a part of Southeast Asia, is also sometimes included.[[39]](#cite_note-Ghosh1989-41)[[41]](#cite_note-Razzaque3-43)[[61]](#cite_note-mittal-63) [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") is also included by some sources.[[39]](#cite_note-Ghosh1989-41)[[41]](#cite_note-Razzaque3-43)[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaez201258-7)

### Organisational definitions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/South_Asia_UN.png/250px-South_Asia_UN.png)](/wiki/File:South_Asia_UN.png)

United Nations map of South Asia.[[63]](#cite_note-unmapsworld-65) However, the United Nations does not endorse any definitions or area boundaries.[[note 2]](#cite_note-66)

The [South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation") (SAARC), a contiguous block of countries, started in 1985 with seven countries – Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, the Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka – and admitted Afghanistan as an eighth member in 2007.[[64]](#cite_note-67)[[37]](#cite_note-SAARC_Summit-39) China and Myanmar have also applied for the status of full members of SAARC.[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69) The [South Asia Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/South_Asia_Free_Trade_Agreement "South Asia Free Trade Agreement") admitted Afghanistan in 2011.[[67]](#cite_note-70)

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and [United Nations Children's Fund](/wiki/United_Nations_Children%27s_Fund "United Nations Children's Fund") (UNICEF) recognises the eight SAARC countries as South Asia,[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-73)[[71]](#cite_note-74) while the [Hirschman–Herfindahl index](/wiki/Herfindahl_index "Herfindahl index") of the [United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Commission_for_Asia_and_the_Pacific "United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific") for the region excludes Afghanistan from South Asia.[[72]](#cite_note-75) Population Information Network (POPIN) excludes Maldives which is included as a member Pacific POPIN subregional network.[[73]](#cite_note-76) The United Nations Statistics Division's [scheme of subregions](/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme "United Nations geoscheme"), for statistical purpose,[[38]](#cite_note-Millenniumindicators.un.org-40) includes [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") along with all eight members of the SAARC as part of [Southern Asia](/wiki/Southern_Asia_(UN_geoscheme) "Southern Asia (UN geoscheme)").[[74]](#cite_note-77)

### Indian subcontinent

See also: [Indian subcontinent § Geopolitics](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent#Geopolitics "Indian subcontinent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Indian_subcontinent.JPG/250px-Indian_subcontinent.JPG)](/wiki/File:Indian_subcontinent.JPG)

The region as described in a 1992 work about the geography of Asia: *"This greater India is well defined in terms of topography; it is the Indian peninsula, hemmed in by the Himalayas on the north, the Hindu Khush in the west and the Arakanese in the east."*[[61]](#cite_note-mittal-63)[[47]](#cite_note-Baker2002-49)

The terms "[Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent")" and "South Asia" are sometimes used interchangeably.[[54]](#cite_note-McLeod2002-56)[[48]](#cite_note-Oxford-50)[[75]](#cite_note-norwineplus-78)[[76]](#cite_note-BoseJalal2004-79)[[77]](#cite_note-80) The Indian subcontinent is largely a geological term referring to the land mass that drifted northeastwards from ancient [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana"), colliding with the Eurasian plate nearly 55 million years ago, towards the end of Palaeocene. This geological region largely includes Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka.[[78]](#cite_note-Jones2011p267-81) Historians Catherine Asher and Cynthia Talbot state that the term "Indian subcontinent" describes a natural physical landmass in South Asia that has been relatively isolated from the rest of Eurasia.[[79]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-82)

The use of the term *Indian subcontinent* began in the British Empire, and has been a term particularly common in its successors.[[75]](#cite_note-norwineplus-78) South Asia as the preferred term is particularly common when scholars or officials seek to differentiate this region from East Asia.[[80]](#cite_note-inden-83) According to historians [Sugata Bose](/wiki/Sugata_Bose "Sugata Bose") and [Ayesha Jalal](/wiki/Ayesha_Jalal "Ayesha Jalal"), the Indian subcontinent has come to be known as South Asia "in more recent and neutral parlance."[[76]](#cite_note-BoseJalal2004-79) This "neutral" notion refers to the concerns of Pakistan and Bangladesh, particularly given the recurring conflicts between India and Pakistan, wherein the dominant placement of "India" as a prefix before the subcontinent might offend some political sentiments.[[61]](#cite_note-mittal-63) In Pakistan, even the term "South Asia" was considered too India-centric and was "banned" within the International Relations department of Karachi University until 1989 after the death of [Zia ul Haq](/wiki/Zia_ul_Haq "Zia ul Haq").[[81]](#cite_note-84) This region has also been labelled as "India" (in its classical and [pre-modern](/wiki/Middle_kingdoms_of_India "Middle kingdoms of India") sense) and "[Greater India](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India")".[[61]](#cite_note-mittal-63)[[47]](#cite_note-Baker2002-49)

### Asian context

See also: [Akhand Bharat](/wiki/Akhand_Bharat "Akhand Bharat") and [Asian relations with Northeast India](/wiki/Asian_relations_with_Northeast_India "Asian relations with Northeast India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Greater_Middle_East_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg/250px-Greater_Middle_East_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greater_Middle_East_(orthographic_projection).svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Central_Asia_definitions_%28orthographic%29.png/250px-Central_Asia_definitions_%28orthographic%29.png)](/wiki/File:Central_Asia_definitions_(orthographic).png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/East_Asia_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg/250px-East_Asia_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:East_Asia_(orthographic_projection).svg)

The [Greater Middle East](/wiki/Greater_Middle_East "Greater Middle East") (top), [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") and associated neighbouring regions (middle), and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") (bottom)[[b]](#cite_note-85)

According to Robert M. Cutler – a scholar of political science at [Carleton University](/wiki/Carleton_University "Carleton University"), the terms South Asia, Southwest Asia, and Central Asia are distinct, but the confusion and disagreements have arisen due to the geopolitical movement to enlarge these regions into Greater South Asia, [Greater Southwest Asia](/wiki/Greater_Middle_East "Greater Middle East"), and [Greater Central Asia](/wiki/Greater_Central_Asia "Greater Central Asia"). The frontier of Greater South Asia, states Cutler, between 2001 and 2006 has been geopolitically extended to eastern Iran and western Afghanistan in the west, and in the north to northeastern Iran, northern Afghanistan, and southern [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan").[[82]](#cite_note-Amineh2007p112-86)

Identification with a South Asian identity was found to be significantly low among respondents in an older two-year survey across Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

## History

For a topical guide, see [Outline of South Asian history](/wiki/Outline_of_South_Asian_history "Outline of South Asian history"). For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of South Asian history](/wiki/Timeline_of_South_Asian_history "Timeline of South Asian history").

For the pre-1947 history of the region, see [History of India](/wiki/History_of_India "History of India"), [History of Pakistan](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan "History of Pakistan"), and [History of Bangladesh](/wiki/History_of_Bangladesh "History of Bangladesh").

### Pre-history

See also: [Peopling of India](/wiki/Peopling_of_India "Peopling of India")

The history of core South Asia begins with evidence of human activity of *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Anatomically_modern_humans "Anatomically modern humans"),* as long as 75,000 years ago, or with earlier hominids including *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* from about 500,000 years ago.[[84]](#cite_note-88) The earliest prehistoric culture have roots in the [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") sites as evidenced by the rock paintings of [Bhimbetka rock shelters](/wiki/Bhimbetka_rock_shelters "Bhimbetka rock shelters") dating to a period of 30,000 BCE or older,[[note 3]](#cite_note-89) as well as Neolithic times.[[note 4]](#cite_note-90)

### Ancient era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Indus_Valley_Civilization%2C_Mature_Phase_%282600-1900_BCE%29.png/250px-Indus_Valley_Civilization%2C_Mature_Phase_%282600-1900_BCE%29.png)](/wiki/File:Indus_Valley_Civilization,_Mature_Phase_(2600-1900_BCE).png)

[Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") during 2600–1900 BCE, the mature phase

The [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation"), which spread and flourished in the [northwestern part](/wiki/Northwestern_South_Asia "Northwestern South Asia") of South Asia from c. 3300 to 1300 BCE in present-day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India"), and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), was the first major civilisation in South Asia.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThapar196623-91) A sophisticated and technologically advanced urban culture developed in the [Mature Harappan](/wiki/Mature_Harappan "Mature Harappan") period, from 2600 to 1900 BCE.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThapar196624-92) According to anthropologist [Possehl](/wiki/Gregory_Possehl "Gregory Possehl"), the Indus Valley civilisation provides a logical, if somewhat arbitrary, starting point for South Asian religions, but these links from the Indus religion to later-day South Asian traditions are subject to scholarly dispute.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPossehl2002141–156-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Mauryan_Empire_ca._265_BCE.png/250px-Mauryan_Empire_ca._265_BCE.png)](/wiki/File:Mauryan_Empire_ca._265_BCE.png)

Maurya Empire in 250 BCE

The Vedic period, named after the Vedic religion of the [Indo-Aryans](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples"),[[note 5]](#cite_note-95) lasted from c. 1900 to 500 BCE.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichaels200432-96)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel19953-4-97) The Indo-Aryans were [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans")-speaking pastoralists[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel1995-98) who migrated into north-western India after the collapse of the Indus Valley Civilization.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichaels200433-94)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood199630–35-99) Linguistic and archaeological data show a cultural change after 1500 BCE,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichaels200433-94) with the linguistic and religious data showing links with Indo-European languages and religion.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood199633-100) By about 1200 BCE, the Vedic culture and agrarian lifestyle were established in the northwest and northern Gangetic plain of South Asia.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel1995-98)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200843–45,_48-101) Rudimentary state-forms appeared, of which the [Kuru](/wiki/Kuru_Kingdom "Kuru Kingdom")-Pañcāla union was the most influential.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel19956-102)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200852–53-103) The first recorded [state-level society](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") in South Asia existed around 1000 BCE.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel1995-98) The Brahmanas and Aranyakas, and the Upanishads into which they merged, began to ask the meaning of a ritual, adding increasing levels of philosophical and metaphysical speculation,[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200825-104) or "[Hindu synthesis](/wiki/Hindu_synthesis "Hindu synthesis")".[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiltebeitel200712-105)

Increasing urbanisation of South Asia between 800 and 400 BCE, and possibly the spread of urban diseases, contributed to the rise of ascetic movements and of new ideas which challenged the orthodox [Brahmanism](/wiki/Brahmanism "Brahmanism").[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood199681–82-106)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] These ideas led to [Sramana](/wiki/Sramana "Sramana") movements, of which Mahavira (c. 549–477 BCE), proponent of [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), and [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") (c. 563 – c. 483), founder of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), was the most prominent icons.[[100]](#cite_note-World_Religions-107)

The Greek army led by [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") stayed in the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") region of South Asia for several years and then later moved into the Indus valley region. Later, the [Maurya Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") extended over much of South Asia in the 3rd century BCE. Buddhism spread beyond south Asia, through northwest into Central Asia. The [Bamiyan Buddhas](/wiki/Bamiyan_Buddhas "Bamiyan Buddhas") of Afghanistan and the [edicts of Aśoka](/wiki/Edicts_of_Ashoka "Edicts of Ashoka") suggest that the Buddhist monks spread Buddhism (Dharma) in eastern provinces of the [Seleucid Empire](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire"), and possibly even farther into West Asia.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGombrich2006135-108)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrainor2004103,_119-109)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeelis2011102–106-110) The Theravada school spread south from India in the 3rd century BCE, to Sri Lanka, later to Southeast Asia.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuy20149–11,_14–15,_19–20-111) Buddhism, by the last centuries of the 1st millennium BCE, was prominent in the Himalayan region, Gandhara, Hindu Kush region, and Bactria.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeelis2011114–115,_144,_160–163,_170–176,_249–250-112)[[106]](#cite_note-deborahkh-113)[[107]](#cite_note-114)

From about 500 BCE through about 300 CE, the Vedic-Brahmanic synthesis or "Hindu synthesis" continued.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiltebeitel200712-105) Classical Hindu and Sramanic (particularly Buddhist) ideas spread within South Asia, as well as outside South Asia.[[108]](#cite_note-115)[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200876–78,_194–201-116)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuy201410–11-117) The [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire") ruled over a large part of the region between the 4th and 7th centuries, a period that saw the construction of major temples, monasteries and universities such as the [Nalanda](/wiki/Nalanda "Nalanda").[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichell197718,_40-118)[[112]](#cite_note-scharfe2002p144-119)[[113]](#cite_note-120) During this era, and through the 10th century, numerous cave monasteries and temples such as the [Ajanta Caves](/wiki/Ajanta_Caves "Ajanta Caves"), [Badami cave temples](/wiki/Badami_cave_temples "Badami cave temples"), and [Ellora Caves](/wiki/Ellora_Caves "Ellora Caves") were built in South Asia.[[114]](#cite_note-121)[[115]](#cite_note-122)[[116]](#cite_note-123)

### Medieval era

See also: [Medieval India](/wiki/Medieval_India "Medieval India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Rajendra_map_new.svg/250px-Rajendra_map_new.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rajendra_map_new.svg)

Outreach of influence of early medieval [Chola dynasty](/wiki/Chola_dynasty "Chola dynasty")

Islam came as a political power in the fringe of South Asia in 8th century CE when the Arab general [Muhammad bin Qasim](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Qasim "Muhammad bin Qasim") conquered [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), and [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan") in southern Punjab, in modern-day Pakistan.[[117]](#cite_note-infopak-124) By 962 CE, Hindu and Buddhist kingdoms in South Asia were under a wave of raids from Muslim armies from Central Asia.[[118]](#cite_note-mrpislam-125) Among them was [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni"), who raided and plundered kingdoms in north India from east of the Indus river to west of Yamuna river seventeen times between 997 and 1030.[[119]](#cite_note-pj03-126) Mahmud of Ghazni raided the treasuries but retracted each time, only extending Islamic rule into western Punjab.[[120]](#cite_note-127)[[121]](#cite_note-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Timur_defeats_the_sultan_of_Delhi.jpg/250px-Timur_defeats_the_sultan_of_Delhi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Timur_defeats_the_sultan_of_Delhi.jpg)

[Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur") defeats the [Sultan of Delhi](/wiki/Sultan_of_Delhi "Sultan of Delhi"), Nasir-u Din Mehmud, in the winter of 1397–1398

The wave of raids on north Indian and western Indian kingdoms by Muslim warlords continued after Mahmud of Ghazni, plundering and looting these kingdoms.[[122]](#cite_note-129) The raids did not establish or extend permanent boundaries of their Islamic kingdoms. The Ghurid Sultan [Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad](/wiki/Mu%27izz_al-Din_Muhammad "Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad") began a systematic war of expansion into North India in 1173.[[123]](#cite_note-130) He sought to carve out a principality for himself by expanding the Islamic world,[[119]](#cite_note-pj03-126)[[124]](#cite_note-131) and thus laid the foundation for the Muslim kingdom that became the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate").[[119]](#cite_note-pj03-126) Some historians chronicle the Delhi Sultanate from 1192 due to the presence and geographical claims of Mu'izz al-Din in South Asia by that time.[[125]](#cite_note-132)

The Delhi Sultanate covered varying parts of South Asia and was ruled by a series of dynasties: Mamluk, Khalji, Tughlaq, Sayyid, and Lodi dynasties. [Muhammad bin Tughlaq](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Tughlaq "Muhammad bin Tughlaq") came to power in 1325, launched a war of expansion and the Delhi Sultanate reached it largest geographical reach over the South Asian region during his 26-year rule.[[126]](#cite_note-ebmit-133) A Sunni Sultan, Muhammad bin Tughlaq persecuted non-Muslims such as Hindus, as well as non-Sunni Muslims such as Shia and Mahdi sects.[[127]](#cite_note-134)[[128]](#cite_note-vsoxfordmbt-135)[[129]](#cite_note-136)

Revolts against the Delhi Sultanate sprang up in many parts of South Asia during the 14th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the northeast, the [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") became independent in 1346 CE. It remained in power through the early 16th century. The state religion of the sultanate was Islam.[[130]](#cite_note-Lewis2011-137)[[131]](#cite_note-Hussain2003-138) In [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India"), the Hindu [Vijayanagara Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire") came to power in 1336 and persisted until the middle of the 16th century. It was ultimately defeated and destroyed by an alliance of the Muslim [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates") at the [Battle of Talikota](/wiki/Battle_of_Talikota "Battle of Talikota").[[132]](#cite_note-139)[[133]](#cite_note-140)

About 1526, the Punjab governor Dawlat Khan Lodī reached out to the Mughal [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") and invited him to attack the Delhi Sultanate. [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") defeated and killed Ibrahim Lodi in the [Battle of Panipat](/wiki/Battle_of_Panipat_(1526) "Battle of Panipat (1526)") in 1526. The death of Ibrahim Lodi ended the Delhi Sultanate, and the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") replaced it.[[134]](#cite_note-eblodi-141)

### Modern era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Emperor_Shah_Jahan_and_Prince_Alamgir_%28Aurangzeb%29_in_Mughal_Court%2C_1650.jpg/250px-Emperor_Shah_Jahan_and_Prince_Alamgir_%28Aurangzeb%29_in_Mughal_Court%2C_1650.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Shah_Jahan_and_Prince_Alamgir_(Aurangzeb)_in_Mughal_Court,_1650.jpg)

Emperor [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan") and his son Prince [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") in Mughal court, 1650

The [modern history period](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era") of South Asia, that is the 16th century onwards, witnessed the establishment of the Mughal Empire, with Sunni Islam theology. The first ruler was Babur had [Turco-Mongol roots](/wiki/Turco-Mongol_tradition "Turco-Mongol tradition") and his realm included the northwestern and [Indo-Gangetic Plain](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain "Indo-Gangetic Plain") regions of South Asia. Several regions of South Asia were largely under [Hindu kings](/wiki/Hindu_king "Hindu king") such as those of the Vijayanagara Empire and the [Kingdom of Mewar](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mewar "Kingdom of Mewar").[[135]](#cite_note-142) Parts of modern [Telangana](/wiki/Telangana "Telangana") and [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") were under local Muslim rulers, the Deccan Sutanates.

The Mughal Empire continued its wars of expansion after Babur's death. With the fall of the Rajput kingdoms and Vijayanagara, its boundaries encompassed almost the entirety of the Indian subcontinent.[[136]](#cite_note-borocz-143) The Mughal Empire was marked by a period of artistic exchanges and a Central Asian and South Asian architecture synthesis, with remarkable buildings such as the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal").[[137]](#cite_note-144)[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaddison2003259–261-145)[[139]](#cite_note-harrison-146)

However, this time also marked an extended period of [religious persecution](/wiki/Religious_violence_in_India#Mughal_Empire "Religious violence in India").[[140]](#cite_note-147) Two of the religious leaders of [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Guru Arjan](/wiki/Guru_Arjan "Guru Arjan"), and [Guru Tegh Bahadur](/wiki/Guru_Tegh_Bahadur "Guru Tegh Bahadur") were arrested under orders of the Mughal emperors after their revolts and were executed when they refused to convert to Islam.[[141]](#cite_note-148)[[142]](#cite_note-149)[[143]](#cite_note-150) Religious taxes on non-Muslims called *[jizya](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya")* were imposed. Buddhist, Hindu, and Sikh temples were desecrated. However, not all Muslim rulers persecuted non-Muslims. [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar"), a Mughal ruler for example, sought religious tolerance and abolished jizya.[[144]](#cite_note-151)[[145]](#cite_note-152)[[146]](#cite_note-153)[[147]](#cite_note-154)

The death of Aurangzeb and the collapse of the Mughal Empire, which marks the beginning of modern India, in the early 18th century, provided opportunities for the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire"), [Mysoreans](/wiki/Mysore_Kingdom "Mysore Kingdom"), and [Nawabs of Bengal](/wiki/Nawabs_of_Bengal "Nawabs of Bengal") to exercise control over large regions of the Indian subcontinent.[[148]](#cite_note-155)[[149]](#cite_note-156) By the mid-18th century, India was a major [proto-industrializing](/wiki/Proto-industrialization "Proto-industrialization") region.[[150]](#cite_note-voss-157)[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaddison2003259–261-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/British_Indian_Empire_1909_Imperial_Gazetteer_of_India.jpg/250px-British_Indian_Empire_1909_Imperial_Gazetteer_of_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Indian_Empire_1909_Imperial_Gazetteer_of_India.jpg)

British Indian Empire in 1909. [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") is shaded pink, the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") are shaded yellow.

Maritime trading between South Asia and European merchants began at the turn of the 16th century after the [Portuguese discovery of the sea route to India](/wiki/Portuguese_discovery_of_the_sea_route_to_India "Portuguese discovery of the sea route to India"). British, French, and Portuguese colonial interests struck treaties with these rulers and established their trading ports. In northwestern South Asia, a large region was consolidated into the Sikh Empire by [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh").[[151]](#cite_note-158)[[152]](#cite_note-159) After the defeat of the [Nawab of Bengal](/wiki/Nawab_of_Bengal "Nawab of Bengal") and [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan") and his [French allies](/wiki/Franco-Indian_Alliances "Franco-Indian Alliances"), [British traders](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") went on to dominate much of South Asia through [divide-and-rule](/wiki/Divide_and_rule "Divide and rule") tactics by the early 19th century. The region experienced significant [de-industrialisation](/wiki/De-industrialisation_of_India "De-industrialisation of India") in its first few decades of British rule.[[153]](#cite_note-:1-160) Control over the subcontinent was then [transferred](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") to the British government after the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), with the British cracking down to some extent afterwards.[[154]](#cite_note-161)

An increase of famines and extreme poverty characterised the colonial period, though railways built with British technology eventually provided crucial famine relief by increasing food distribution throughout India.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697-162)[[153]](#cite_note-:1-160) Millions of South Asians began to migrate [throughout the world](/wiki/South_Asian_diaspora#Colonial_era "South Asian diaspora"), impelled by the economic/[labour](/wiki/Indian_indenture_system "Indian indenture system") needs and opportunities presented by the British Empire.[[156]](#cite_note-163)[[157]](#cite_note-164)[[158]](#cite_note-165) The introduction of [Western political thought](/wiki/Western_values_(West) "Western values (West)") inspired a growing Indian intellectual movement, and so by the 20th century, British rule began to be challenged by the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"), which sought full independence under the leadership of [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi").[[159]](#cite_note-D_Demy_2017_p._644-166)[[160]](#cite_note-167)

Britain, under pressure from Indian freedom fighters, increasingly gave self-rule to British India. By the 1940s, two rival camps had emerged among independence activists: those who [favored a separate nation](/wiki/Muslim_nationalism_in_South_Asia "Muslim nationalism in South Asia") for Indian Muslims, and those who [wanted a united India](/wiki/Composite_nationalism "Composite nationalism"). As [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") raged, over 2 million Indians fought for Britain;[[161]](#cite_note-Darwin2011-168) by the end of the war, Britain was greatly weakened, and thus decided to grant independence to the vast majority of South Asians in 1947,[[161]](#cite_note-Darwin2011-168)[[162]](#cite_note-169) though this coincided with the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") into a Hindu-majority India and a Muslim-majority Pakistan, which resulted in significant displacement and violence and harder religious divides in the region.[[163]](#cite_note-170)

### Contemporary era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/India_%26_Pakistan_on_eve_of_Independence.png/120px-India_%26_Pakistan_on_eve_of_Independence.png)](/wiki/File:India_%26_Pakistan_on_eve_of_Independence.png)

Indian (brown) and Pakistani (green) territory on 15 August 1947, upon independence.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/India_Pakistan_China_Disputed_Areas_Map.png/120px-India_Pakistan_China_Disputed_Areas_Map.png)](/wiki/File:India_Pakistan_China_Disputed_Areas_Map.png)

India and Pakistan consolidated their territory in the mid-20th century, but have an ongoing dispute over [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir").

In 1947, the newly independent India and Pakistan had to decide how to deal with the hundreds of [princely states](/wiki/Princely_states "Princely states") that controlled much of the subcontinent, as well as what to do with the remaining European (non-British) colonies.[[164]](#cite_note-171) A combination of referendums, military action, and negotiated accessions took place in rapid succession, leading to the political integration of the [vast majority of India](/wiki/Political_integration_of_India "Political integration of India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Princely_states_of_Pakistan "Princely states of Pakistan") within a few years.[[165]](#cite_note-172)[[166]](#cite_note-173)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/India_and_Pakistan_November_1971.png/250px-India_and_Pakistan_November_1971.png)](/wiki/File:India_and_Pakistan_November_1971.png)

South Asia on the eve of the de jure commencement of the [1971 Indo-Pakistani war](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_war_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani war of 1971"), which concluded with [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan") becoming the independent nation of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").

India and Pakistan [clashed several times](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_wars_and_conflicts "Indo-Pakistani wars and conflicts") in the decades after Independence, with [disputes over Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") playing a significant role.[[167]](#cite_note-174) In 1971, the eastern half of Pakistan seceded with help from India and became the People's Republic of Bangladesh after the traumatic [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War").[[168]](#cite_note-175) This, along with India and Pakistan gaining nuclear weapons soon afterwards, increased tensions between the two countries.[[169]](#cite_note-176) The [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") decades also [contributed to the divide](/wiki/Cold_War_in_Asia#South_Asia "Cold War in Asia"), as Pakistan aligned with the West and India with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union");[[170]](#cite_note-177)[[171]](#cite_note-178) other factors include the time period after the 1962 [Sino-Indian War](/wiki/Sino-Indian_War "Sino-Indian War"), which saw India and China move apart while Pakistan and China built closer relations.[[172]](#cite_note-179)

Pakistan has been beset with [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism_in_Pakistan "Terrorism in Pakistan"), [economic issues](/wiki/Poverty_in_Pakistan "Poverty in Pakistan"), and [military dominance of its government](/wiki/Military_coups_in_Pakistan "Military coups in Pakistan") since Independence,[[173]](#cite_note-180) with none of its Prime Ministers having completed a full 5-year term in office.[[174]](#cite_note-181) India has grown significantly,[[175]](#cite_note-182) having slashed its rate of extreme poverty to below 20%[[176]](#cite_note-:2-183) and surpassed Pakistan's GDP per capita in the 2010s due to [economic liberalisation](/wiki/Economic_liberalisation_in_India "Economic liberalisation in India") from the 1980s onward.[[177]](#cite_note-:4-184) Bangladesh, having struggled greatly for decades due to conflict with and economic exploitation by Pakistan,[[178]](#cite_note-185)[[179]](#cite_note-186) is now one of the fastest-growing countries in the region, beating India in terms of [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita").[[180]](#cite_note-187)[[181]](#cite_note-188) Afghanistan has gone through [several invasions](/wiki/Invasions_of_Afghanistan#Soviet_invasions:_1929,_1930,_and_1979 "Invasions of Afghanistan") and Islamist regimes, with many of its refugees having gone to Pakistan and other parts of South Asia and bringing back cultural influences such as [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket").[[182]](#cite_note-189)[[183]](#cite_note-:3-190) [Religious nationalism](/wiki/Religious_nationalism "Religious nationalism") has grown across the region,[[184]](#cite_note-191)[[185]](#cite_note-dip21Aug2023-192) with persecution causing millions of Hindus and Christians to flee Pakistan and Bangladesh,[[186]](#cite_note-193)[[187]](#cite_note-194) and [Hindu nationalism](/wiki/Hindutva "Hindutva") having grown in India with the election of the [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") in 2014.[[185]](#cite_note-dip21Aug2023-192)

A recent phenomenon has been that of India and China [fighting on their border](/wiki/Sino-Indian_border_dispute "Sino-Indian border dispute"), as well as vying for [dominance of South Asia](/wiki/String_of_Pearls_(Indian_Ocean) "String of Pearls (Indian Ocean)"), with China partnering with Pakistan and using its superior economy to attract countries surrounding India, while [America and other countries](/wiki/Quadrilateral_Security_Dialogue "Quadrilateral Security Dialogue") have strengthened ties with India to counter China in the broader [Indo-Pacific](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific").[[188]](#cite_note-195)[[189]](#cite_note-196) (See also: [2025 India–Pakistan standoff](/wiki/2025_India%E2%80%93Pakistan_standoff "2025 India–Pakistan standoff"))

## Geography

See also: [Indian subcontinent § Geography](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent#Geography "Indian subcontinent")

Further information: [Geography of India](/wiki/Geography_of_India "Geography of India"), [Geography of Pakistan](/wiki/Geography_of_Pakistan "Geography of Pakistan"), [Geography of Afghanistan](/wiki/Geography_of_Afghanistan "Geography of Afghanistan"), [Geography of Bangladesh](/wiki/Geography_of_Bangladesh "Geography of Bangladesh"), [Geography of Bhutan](/wiki/Geography_of_Bhutan "Geography of Bhutan"), [Geography of Sri Lanka](/wiki/Geography_of_Sri_Lanka "Geography of Sri Lanka"), [Geography of Nepal](/wiki/Geography_of_Nepal "Geography of Nepal"), and [Geography of the Maldives](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Maldives "Geography of the Maldives")

According to Saul Cohen, early colonial era strategists treated South Asia with East Asia, but in reality, the South Asia region excluding Afghanistan is a distinct [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitics "Geopolitics") region separated from other nearby geostrategic realms, one that is geographically diverse.[[190]](#cite_note-197) The region is home to a variety of geographical features, such as [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier"), [rainforests](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest"), [valleys](/wiki/Valley "Valley"), [deserts](/wiki/Desert "Desert"), and [grasslands](/wiki/Grassland "Grassland") that are typical of much larger continents. It is surrounded by three water bodies – the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"), the Indian Ocean and the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") – and has acutely varied climate zones. The tip of the Indian Peninsula had the highest quality pearls.[[191]](#cite_note-198)

### Indian Plate

Main article: [Indian Plate](/wiki/Indian_Plate "Indian Plate")

Most of this region is resting on the [Indian Plate](/wiki/Indian_Plate "Indian Plate"), the northerly portion of the [Indo-Australian Plate](/wiki/Indo-Australian_Plate "Indo-Australian Plate"), separated from the rest of the [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate"). The Indian Plate includes most of South Asia, forming a land mass which extends from the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") into a portion of the basin under the Indian Ocean, including parts of [South China](/wiki/Southwest_China "Southwest China") and Eastern [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), as well as [Kunlun](/wiki/Kunlun_Mountains "Kunlun Mountains") and [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram") ranges,[[192]](#cite_note-199)[[193]](#cite_note-200) and extending up to but not including [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh"), [Kohistan](/wiki/Kohistan_District,_Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa "Kohistan District, Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa"), the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") range, and [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan "Balochistan").[[194]](#cite_note-201)[[195]](#cite_note-202)[[196]](#cite_note-203) It may be noted that [geophysically](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics") the [Yarlung Tsangpo River](/wiki/Yarlung_Tsangpo_River "Yarlung Tsangpo River") in Tibet is situated at the outside the border of the regional structure, while the [Pamir Mountains](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") in Tajikistan are situated inside that border.[[197]](#cite_note-204)

The [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") formerly formed part of the [supercontinent](/wiki/Supercontinent "Supercontinent") [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana"), before rifting away during the [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") period and colliding with the Eurasian Plate about 50–55 million years ago and giving birth to the [Himalayan range](/wiki/Himalayan_range "Himalayan range") and the [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau"). It is the [peninsular](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") region south of the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalaya "Himalaya") and [Kuen Lun](/wiki/Kuen_Lun "Kuen Lun") mountain ranges and east of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") and the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau"), extending southward into the Indian Ocean between the Arabian Sea (to the southwest) and the Bay of Bengal (to the southeast).

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/South_Asia_map_of_K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification.svg/500px-South_Asia_map_of_K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification.svg.png)](/wiki/File:South_Asia_map_of_K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification.svg)

South Asia's [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map[[198]](#cite_note-Peel-205) is based on native vegetation, temperature, precipitation, and their seasonality.

[(Af) **Tropical rainforest**](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate")

[(Am) **Tropical monsoon**](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate")

[(Aw) **Tropical savanna**, wet & dry](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate")

[(BWh) **Hot desert**](/wiki/Hot_desert_climate "Hot desert climate")

[(BWk) **Cold desert**](/wiki/Cold_desert_climate "Cold desert climate")

[(BSh) **Hot semi arid**](/wiki/Hot_semi-arid_climate "Hot semi-arid climate")

[(BSk) **Cold semi arid**](/wiki/Cold_semi-arid_climate "Cold semi-arid climate")

[(Csa) **Mediterranean**, dry, hot summer](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate")

[(Cfa) **Subtropical**, humid](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate")

[(Cwa) **Subtropical**, humid summer, dry winter](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate")

[(Cwb) **Subtropical highland**, dry winter](/wiki/Subtropical_highland_climate "Subtropical highland climate")

[(Dsa) **Continental**, hot summer](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate")

[(Dsb) **Continental**, warm summer](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate")

[(Dwb) **Continental**, dry winter](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate")

[(Dwc) **Continental Subarctic**, dry winter](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate")

The climate of this vast region varies considerably from area to area from tropical monsoon in the south to temperate in the north. The variety is influenced by not only the altitude but also by factors such as proximity to the seacoast and the seasonal impact of the [monsoons](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon"). Southern parts are mostly hot in summers and receive rain during monsoon periods. The northern belt of [Indo-Gangetic plains](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_plain "Indo-Gangetic plain") also is hot in summer, but cooler in winter. The mountainous north is colder and receives snowfall at higher altitudes of Himalayan ranges.

As the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") block the north-Asian bitter cold winds, the temperatures are considerably moderate in the plains down below. For the most part, the climate of the region is called the monsoon climate, which keeps the region humid during summer and dry during winter, and favours the cultivation of [jute](/wiki/Jute "Jute"), tea, rice, and various vegetables in this region.

South Asia is largely divided into four broad climate zones:[[199]](#cite_note-olive1-206)

* The northern Indian edge and northern Pakistani uplands have a dry subtropical [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate")
* The far south of India and southwest Sri Lanka have an [equatorial climate](/wiki/Equatorial_climate "Equatorial climate")
* Most of the peninsula has a tropical climate with variations:
  + Hot [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropics "Subtropics") climate in northwest India
  + Cool winter hot [tropical climate](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") in Bangladesh
  + Tropical [semi-arid climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") in the center
* The Himalayas and most of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") have an [Alpine climate](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate")

Maximum relative humidity of over 80% has been recorded in [Khasi and Jaintia Hills](/wiki/Khasi_and_Jaintia_Hills "Khasi and Jaintia Hills") and Sri Lanka, while the area adjustment to Pakistan and western India records lower than 20%–30%.[[199]](#cite_note-olive1-206) Climate of South Asia is largely characterised by monsoons. South Asia depends critically on monsoon rainfall.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitraKumarKumarAbrol200283-207) Two monsoon systems exist in the region:[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitraKumarKumarAbrol200276-208)

* The summer monsoon: Wind blows from the southwest to most parts of the region. It accounts for 70%–90% of the annual precipitation.
* The winter monsoon: Wind blows from the northeast. Dominant in Sri Lanka and Maldives.

The warmest period of the year precedes the monsoon season (March to mid June). In the summer the low pressures are centered over the [Indus-Gangetic Plain](/wiki/Indus-Gangetic_Plain "Indus-Gangetic Plain") and high wind from the Indian Ocean blows towards the center. The monsoons are the second coolest season of the year because of high humidity and cloud covering. But, at the beginning of June, the jetstreams vanish above the [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau"), low pressure over the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley "Indus Valley") deepens and the [Intertropical Convergence Zone](/wiki/Intertropical_Convergence_Zone "Intertropical Convergence Zone") (ITCZ) moves in. The change is violent. Moderately vigorous monsoon depressions form in the Bay of Bengal and make landfall from June to September.[[199]](#cite_note-olive1-206)

#### Climate change

Main article: [Climate change in Asia](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Asia "Climate change in Asia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Guo_2023_Asia_IVT_projections.jpg/250px-Guo_2023_Asia_IVT_projections.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guo_2023_Asia_IVT_projections.jpg)

Greater warming increases the amount of moisture in the atmosphere over Asia, which directly leads to extreme precipitation. Probability of 20-year, 50-year and 100-year extremes consistently increases with warming across Asia. In the most affected parts of South Asia, up to a 15-fold average increase for 100-year extremes is possible under 3 °C (5.4 °F) of warming.[[202]](#cite_note-Guo2023-209)

South Asian countries are expected to experience more flooding in the future as the monsoon pattern intensifies.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1459 Across Asia as a whole, 100-year extremes in vapour transport (directly related to extreme precipitation) would become 2.6 times more frequent under 1.5 °C (2.7 °F) of global warming, yet 3.9 and 7.5 times more frequent under 2 °C (3.6 °F) and 3 °C (5.4 °F). In parts of South Asia, they could become up to 15 times more frequent.[[202]](#cite_note-Guo2023-209) At the same time, up to two-thirds of glacier ice in the Hindu Kush region may melt by 2100 under high warming, and these glaciers feed the water basin of over 220 million people.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1487 As glacier meltwater flow diminishes after 2050, [Hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") generation would become less predictable and reliable, while agriculture would become more reliant on the intensified monsoon than ever before.[[204]](#cite_note-:HKH2019Climate-211)[[205]](#cite_note-:HKH2019Cryo-212)[[206]](#cite_note-:HKH2019Water-213) Around 2050, people living in the [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges") and [Indus](/wiki/Indus "Indus") river basins (where up to 60% of non-monsoon irrigation comes from the glaciers[[207]](#cite_note-Biemans2019-214)) may be faced with severe water scarcity due to both climate and socioeconomic reasons.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1486

By 2030, major Indian cities such as [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), [Cuttack](/wiki/Cuttack "Cuttack"), and [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") are expected to end up with much of their territory below the tide level.[[208]](#cite_note-215) In Mumbai alone, failing to adapt to this would result in damages of US$112–162 billion by 2050, which would nearly triple by 2070.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210) [Sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") in Bangladesh will displace 0.9–2.1 million people by 2050 and may force the relocation of up to one third of power plants by 2030.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210) [Food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security") will become more uneven, and some South Asian countries could experience significant social impacts from global food price volatility.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1494 [Infectious diarrhoea](/wiki/Infectious_diarrhoea "Infectious diarrhoea") mortality and [dengue fever](/wiki/Dengue_fever "Dengue fever") incidence is also likely to increase across South Asia.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1508 Parts of South Asia would also reach "critical health thresholds" for [heat stress](/wiki/Heat_stress "Heat stress") under all but the lowest-emission [climate change scenarios](/wiki/Climate_change_scenario "Climate change scenario").[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1465 Under the high-emission scenario, 40 million people (nearly 2% of the South Asian population) may be driven to internal migration by 2050 due to climate change.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1469

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/HKH-Glacier-Mass-Change.png/250px-HKH-Glacier-Mass-Change.png)](/wiki/File:HKH-Glacier-Mass-Change.png)

Observed glacier mass loss in the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") region since the 20th century[[205]](#cite_note-:HKH2019Cryo-212)

India is estimated to have the world's highest [social cost of carbon](/wiki/Social_cost_of_carbon "Social cost of carbon") - meaning that it experiences the greatest impact from [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions").[[209]](#cite_note-Guardian-216) Other estimates describe Bangladesh as the country most likely to be the worst-affected.[[210]](#cite_note-217)[[211]](#cite_note-218)[[212]](#cite_note-219) In the 2017 edition of [Germanwatch](/wiki/Germanwatch "Germanwatch")'s *Climate Risk Index*, Bangladesh and Pakistan ranked sixth and seventh respectively as the countries most affected by climate change in the period from 1996 to 2015, while India ranked fourth among the list of countries most affected by climate change in 2015.[[213]](#cite_note-220) Some research suggests that South Asia as a whole would lose 2% of its [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") to climate change by 2050, while these losses would approach 9% by the end of the century under the most intense climate change scenario.[[203]](#cite_note-AR6_WGII_Chapter10-210): 1468

### Regions

Main category: [Regions of South Asia](/wiki/Category:Regions_of_South_Asia "Category:Regions of South Asia")

#### Northern South Asia

[Ethnolinguistically](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group"), [Northern South Asia](/wiki/Northern_South_Asia "Northern South Asia") is predominantly [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples"),[[214]](#cite_note-221)[[215]](#cite_note-222) along with [Iranic populations](/wiki/Iranic_peoples "Iranic peoples") in Afghanistan and [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan "Balochistan"), and diverse linguistic communities near the Himalayas.[[216]](#cite_note-223)[[217]](#cite_note-224) Its borders have been heavily contested (primarily [between India](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_of_India "Territorial disputes of India") and its neighbours [Pakistan](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_border_dispute "Indo-Pakistani border dispute") and [China](/wiki/Sino-Indian_border_dispute "Sino-Indian border dispute"), as well as [separatist movements](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Northeast_India "Insurgency in Northeast India") in Northeast India.)[[218]](#cite_note-225)[[219]](#cite_note-:03-226)[[220]](#cite_note-227) This tension in the region has contributed to difficulties in sharing river waters among Northern South Asian countries;[[221]](#cite_note-228) climate change is projected to exacerbate the issue.[[222]](#cite_note-229)[[223]](#cite_note-230) Dominated by the [Indo-Gangetic Plain](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain "Indo-Gangetic Plain"), the region is home to about half a billion people and is the poorest South Asian subregion.[[219]](#cite_note-:03-226)

#### Northwestern South Asia

[Northwestern South Asia](/wiki/Northwestern_South_Asia "Northwestern South Asia") is the site of many of the first civilisations of the world, such as the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation").[[224]](#cite_note-231)[[225]](#cite_note-232) It was historically the most-conquered region of South Asia because it is the first region that invading armies coming from the west had to cross to enter the Indian subcontinent;[[226]](#cite_note-233) because of these many invasions, Northwestern South Asia has significant influences from various sources outside of South Asia, mainly from the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world").[[227]](#cite_note-234)

#### Eastern South Asia

[Eastern South Asia](/wiki/Eastern_South_Asia "Eastern South Asia") includes [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") ([East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal")), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), [eastern](/wiki/East_India "East India") and [northeastern](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India") parts of India, and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"). Major Hindu and Buddhist kingdoms and empires of the region include [Kikata](/wiki/Kikata_kingdom "Kikata kingdom"), [Videha](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Videhas "Kingdom of the Videhas"), [Vṛji](/wiki/Vajjika_League "Vajjika League"), [Magadha](/wiki/Magadha_(Mahajanapada) "Magadha (Mahajanapada)"), [Nanda](/wiki/Nanda_Empire "Nanda Empire"), [Gangaridai](/wiki/Gangaridai "Gangaridai"), [Mauryan](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire"), [Anga](/wiki/Anga "Anga"), [Kalinga](/wiki/Kalinga_(historical_region) "Kalinga (historical region)"), [Kamarupa](/wiki/Kamarupa "Kamarupa"), [Samatata](/wiki/Samatata "Samatata"), [Kanva](/wiki/Kanva_dynasty "Kanva dynasty"), [Gupta](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire"), [Pala](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire"), [Gauda](/wiki/Gauda_kingdom "Gauda kingdom"), [Sena](/wiki/Sena_Empire "Sena Empire"), [Khadga](/wiki/Khadga_dynasty "Khadga dynasty"), [Candra](/wiki/Candra_dynasty "Candra dynasty"), and [Deva](/wiki/Deva_dynasty "Deva dynasty"). Geographically, it lies between the [Eastern Himalayas](/wiki/Eastern_Himalaya "Eastern Himalaya") and the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"). Two of the world's largest rivers, the [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges") and the [Brahmaputra](/wiki/Brahmaputra "Brahmaputra"), flow into the sea through the [Bengal region](/wiki/Bengal_region "Bengal region"). The region includes the world's largest delta, the [Ganges Delta](/wiki/Ganges_Delta "Ganges Delta"), and has a climate ranging from [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") and [subalpine](/wiki/Montane_ecosystems#Subalpine_zone "Montane ecosystems") to [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropics "Subtropics") and [tropical](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics"). Since Nepal, Bhutan, Northeast India and parts of East India are [landlocked](/wiki/Landlocked_country "Landlocked country"), the coastlines of Bangladesh and East India (in [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") and [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha")) serve as the principal gateways to the region. With more than 441 million inhabitants, Eastern South Asia is home to 6% of the [world's population](/wiki/World_population "World population") and 25% of South Asia's population.

#### Central-West Coastal South Asia

This section is an excerpt from [Western India § History](/wiki/Western_India#History "Western India").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Western_India&action=edit)]

Parts of Gujarat were the site of the [Indus Valley civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilisation "Indus Valley civilisation"). Places have been uncovered in Gujarat at [Lothal](/wiki/Lothal "Lothal"), [Surkotada](/wiki/Surkotada "Surkotada"), and around [Ghaggar](/wiki/Ghaggar "Ghaggar") River in [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"). The Western Indian region was ruled by the [Rashtrakuta](/wiki/Rashtrakuta "Rashtrakuta") Empire, the [Maurya](/wiki/Maurya "Maurya") Empire, the [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire"), [Rajputs](/wiki/Rajputs "Rajputs"), [Satavahanas](/wiki/Satavahanas "Satavahanas"), [Western Satraps](/wiki/Western_Satraps "Western Satraps"), [Indo Greeks](/wiki/Indo_Greeks "Indo Greeks"), [Kadambas](/wiki/Kadambas_of_Goa "Kadambas of Goa") etc. in the ancients times. During the medieval age, the area was under the rule of the [Vaghela dynasty](/wiki/Vaghela_dynasty "Vaghela dynasty"), the [Gujarat Sultanate](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate"), and the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"). Thereafter, the area was under [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") rule. Later, the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), which arose in western Maharashtra, came to dominate a major portion of the Indian sub-continent. However, its defeat by the British in the [Anglo-Maratha](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Maratha_War "Third Anglo-Maratha War") wars left most of India under colonial rule. The region then experienced great upheavals during the struggle for [Indian Independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"). [Gandhi](/wiki/Gandhi "Gandhi")'s [Dandi March](/wiki/Dandi_March "Dandi March") took place in Gujarat. The region became part of independent India in 1947, and the present state boundaries were drawn based on linguistic considerations in 1956.[[228]](#cite_note-Western_India_states_reorganization_act-235)

#### Central South Asia

This section is an excerpt from [Central India § History](/wiki/Central_India#History "Central India").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Central_India&action=edit)]

The most powerful and greatest dynasty to emerge from Central India was undoubtedly the [Chandelas of Jejakabhukti](/wiki/Chandelas_of_Jejakabhukti "Chandelas of Jejakabhukti"). Apart from their extensive conquests, Chandelas played a more important in the national struggle against [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") invaders. They ruled Central India for about six centuries, a period that is rather unusual in the annals of the ruling dynasties of [India](/wiki/India "India").[[229]](#cite_note-236)

The [Holkars](/wiki/Holkars "Holkars") of [Indore](/wiki/Indore "Indore"), [Scindias](/wiki/Scindia "Scindia") of [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior "Gwalior"), [Puars](/wiki/Pawar "Pawar") of [Dewas Junior](/wiki/Dewas_Junior "Dewas Junior"), [Dewas Senior](/wiki/Dewas_Senior "Dewas Senior") and [Dhar State](/wiki/Dhar_State "Dhar State") were powerful families of the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") which were based in Central India. The territories that now comprises [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh") and [Chhattisgarh](/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh") were ruled by numerous [princes](/wiki/Princely_State "Princely State") who entered into [subsidiary alliance](/wiki/Subsidiary_alliance "Subsidiary alliance") with the [British](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").

#### Southern South Asia

[Southern South Asia](/wiki/Southern_South_Asia "Southern South Asia") includes [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India"), the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"),[[230]](#cite_note-237)[[231]](#cite_note-:05-238) and is predominantly [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_peoples "Dravidian peoples").[[232]](#cite_note-239)[[233]](#cite_note-240) Southern South Asia was a hub of global trade in ancient times because of its position in the important Indian Ocean corridor.[[234]](#cite_note-241)

Historically, governments throughout Southern South Asia adopted Sanskrit for public political expression beginning around 300 CE and ending around 1300, resulting in greater integration into the broader [South Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India").[[235]](#cite_note-242) This significantly influenced the languages of the region, making all of the major Dravidian languages except for Tamil highly [Sanskritised](/wiki/Sanskritisation_(linguistics) "Sanskritisation (linguistics)").[[236]](#cite_note-243)[[237]](#cite_note-244) Tamil influence in the region is quite significant, with prominent empires such as the [Chola dynasty](/wiki/Chola_dynasty "Chola dynasty") taking [Tamil culture](/wiki/Tamil_culture "Tamil culture") to Sri Lanka and beyond South Asia, and Sri Lanka having an ancient [Tamil minority](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Tamils "Sri Lankan Tamils") and a Dravidian-influenced majority language of [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language").[[238]](#cite_note-245)[[239]](#cite_note-246)[[240]](#cite_note-247)

## Society

See also: [South Asian ethnic groups](/wiki/South_Asian_ethnic_groups "South Asian ethnic groups")

### Population

The population of South Asia is about 1.938 billion which makes it the [most populated region](/wiki/List_of_continents_by_population#Regional_and_continental_(sub)totals_in_2013 "List of continents by population") in the world.[[241]](#cite_note-248) It is socially very mixed, consisting of many language groups and religions, and social practices in one region that are vastly different from those in another.[[242]](#cite_note-249)
